title: Art name

infobox.value: ごう ([modern usage](/wiki/Modern_kana_usage "Modern kana usage")) がう ([historical usage](/wiki/Historical_kana_usage "Historical kana usage"))

This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. (**pseudonym** or **pen name**), also known by its native names ***hào*** (in [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese")), ***gō*** (in Japanese), ****ho**** (in Korean), and ***tên hiệu*** (in Vietnamese), is a professional name used by [artists](/wiki/Artist "Artist"), poets and writers in the [Sinosphere](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere"). The word and the concept originated in [China](/wiki/China "China"), where it was used as nicknames for the educated, then became popular in other [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian "East Asian") countries (especially in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), and the former [Kingdom of Ryukyu](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ryukyu "Kingdom of Ryukyu")).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Professional name used by East Asian artists

| Art name | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
|  | |

| [Simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters") | 号 |
| Literal meaning | "mark" |
| | Transcriptions | | | --- | --- | | [Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") | | | [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin") | hào | | [Wade–Giles](/wiki/Wade%E2%80%93Giles "Wade–Giles") | hao | | |
| Vietnamese name | |
| [Vietnamese alphabet](/wiki/Vietnamese_alphabet "Vietnamese alphabet") | *tên hiệu* |
| [Hán-Nôm](/wiki/H%C3%A1n-N%C3%B4m "Hán-Nôm") | 𠸛號 |

| Japanese name | |
| [Kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana") | ごう ([modern usage](/wiki/Modern_kana_usage "Modern kana usage")) がう ([historical usage](/wiki/Historical_kana_usage "Historical kana usage")) |
| [Kyūjitai](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABjitai "Kyūjitai") | 號 |

|  | |

An **art name** (**pseudonym** or **pen name**), also known by its native names ***hào*** (in [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese")), ***gō*** (in Japanese), ****ho**** (in Korean), and ***tên hiệu*** (in Vietnamese), is a professional name used by [artists](/wiki/Artist "Artist"), poets and writers in the [Sinosphere](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere"). The word and the concept originated in [China](/wiki/China "China"), where it was used as nicknames for the educated, then became popular in other [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian "East Asian") countries (especially in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), and the former [Kingdom of Ryukyu](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ryukyu "Kingdom of Ryukyu")).

In some cases, artists adopted different pseudonyms at different stages of their career, usually to mark significant changes in their life. Extreme practitioners of this tendency were [Tang Yin](/wiki/Tang_Yin "Tang Yin") of the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), who had more than ten *hao*, [Hokusai](/wiki/Hokusai "Hokusai") of Japan, who in the period 1798 to 1806 alone used no fewer than six, and [Kim Chŏnghŭi](/wiki/Kim_Ch%C5%8Fngh%C5%ADi "Kim Chŏnghŭi") of the [Joseon Dynasty](/wiki/Joseon_Dynasty "Joseon Dynasty") who had up to 503.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_name&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_name&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: China")]

In Chinese culture, *Hao* refers to honorific names made by oneself or given by others when one is in middle age. After one's gaining the *Hao*, other persons may then call such a person by one's *Hao* even without such a person being presented.[[note 1]](#cite_note-2) *Hao* usually is made by a person oneself, but sometimes is given by a high-ranked official or even is bestowed by the monarch.[[2]](#cite_note-liu-3)

The use of this name as a nom de plume or artistic name, however, appears to have begun only during the [Six Dynasties](/wiki/Six_Dynasties "Six Dynasties") period, with [Tao Yuanming](/wiki/Tao_Yuanming "Tao Yuanming") and [Ge Hong](/wiki/Ge_Hong "Ge Hong") among the first literati to have given themselves *Hao*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Art names came into vogue during the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), during which time they could either be coined by the persons themselves, or given to them as a name by others. Most *Hao* can be placed within a few categories:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* *Hao* derived from the locations or characteristics of the person's residence. For instance, Tao Yuanming was Wuliu Xiansheng, "Mister Five-Willows", while [Su Shi](/wiki/Su_Shi "Su Shi") was Dongpo Jushi, "[Householder](/wiki/Householder_(Buddhism) "Householder (Buddhism)") of the Eastern Slope", after his residence while exiled in [Huangzhou](/wiki/Huangzhou "Huangzhou"). These were mostly self-coined.
* *Hao* derived from certain well known sayings by the person. For example, [Ouyang Xiu](/wiki/Ouyang_Xiu "Ouyang Xiu") was known as Liuyi Jushi, "Householder of the Six Ones", after his self description as "One myriad books, one thousand inscriptions, one [qin](/wiki/Guqin "Guqin"), one game of chess, one flask of wine and one old man".
* *Hao* derived from one's famous poetic lines or images. These were most often given by others in admiration. [Li Bai](/wiki/Li_Bai "Li Bai"), for his free-spirited behaviour, was known as Zhe Xianren, "Banished Immortal"; while the poet [He Zhu](/w/index.php?title=He_Zhu&action=edit&redlink=1 "He Zhu (page does not exist)") was known as He Meizi, "He the Plum", after an acclaimed line about yellow plums.
* *Hao* derived from one's official posts, birthplace, or a place where they served as officials. [Du Fu](/wiki/Du_Fu "Du Fu") was known as Du Gongbu, "Du of the Ministry of Works", having briefly been a senior officer in that ministry. [Tang Xianzu](/wiki/Tang_Xianzu "Tang Xianzu") was called "Tang of Nanhai" for his birthplace.

By the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), the majority of literati called each other by their art names, which in turn often changed; this situation continued up to the 20th century.

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_name&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Japan")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Art_name "Special:EditPage/Art name") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(September 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In [early modern](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") Japan, a [woodblock print](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") artist's first *gō* was usually given to them by the head of the school (a group of artists and apprentices, with a senior as master of the school) in which they initially studied; this *gō* usually included one of the characters of the master's *gō*. For example, one of Hokusai's earliest pseudonyms was Shunrō; his master [Katsukawa Shunshō](/wiki/Katsukawa_Shunsh%C5%8D "Katsukawa Shunshō") having granted him the character 'shun' from his own name.

One can often trace the relationship among artists with this, especially in later years, when it seems to have been fairly (although not uniformly) systematic (particularly in the [Utagawa school](/wiki/Utagawa_school "Utagawa school")) that the first character of the pupil's *gō* was the last of the master's *gō*.

Thus, an artist named [Toyoharu](/wiki/Utagawa_Toyoharu "Utagawa Toyoharu") had a student named [Toyohiro](/wiki/Utagawa_Toyohiro "Utagawa Toyohiro"), who, in turn, had as a pupil the famous landscape artist [Hiroshige](/wiki/Hiroshige "Hiroshige").

Another figure who studied under Toyoharu was the principal head of the [Utagawa school](/wiki/Utagawa_school "Utagawa school"), [Toyokuni](/wiki/Utagawa_Toyokuni "Utagawa Toyokuni"). Toyokuni had pupils named [Kunisada](/wiki/Utagawa_Kunisada "Utagawa Kunisada") and [Kuniyoshi](/wiki/Utagawa_Kuniyoshi "Utagawa Kuniyoshi"). Kuniyoshi, in turn, had as a student [Yoshitoshi](/wiki/Utagawa_Yoshitoshi "Utagawa Yoshitoshi"), whose pupils included Toshikata.

### Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_name&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Korea")]

In Korea, these names are called *ho* ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"): 호; [Hanja](/wiki/Hanja "Hanja"): 號). According to the *[Encyclopedia of Korean Culture](/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Korean_Culture "Encyclopedia of Korean Culture"),* they arrived on the peninsula from China during the [Three Kingdoms of Korea](/wiki/Three_Kingdoms_of_Korea "Three Kingdoms of Korea") period (57 BC – 668 AD). They are now relatively uncommon, although some recent authors use [pen names](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name") (필명; 筆名; *pilmyeong*), which are seen as very similar to a subvariant of *ho* called *aho*.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) During deliberations of the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Korea "Constitutional Court of Korea"), justices address one another by their *ho*, rather than by personal names.[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)

People can either create their own *ho* or it can be given to them by others. Typically people select their own *ho* based on the meaning of the name. When other people give a person a *ho*, it typically reflects their shared social context or relationship (parent to child, friend to friend, teacher to student, etc). Names can also be selected to avoid [naming taboos](/wiki/Naming_taboo "Naming taboo").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

While most *ho* are made of two characters, they can be of any length. For example, the poet [Kim Sang-ok](/wiki/Kim_Sang-ok "Kim Sang-ok") had one that is ten characters long (칠수삼과처용지거주인; 七須三瓜處容之居主人). A person can also have any number of *ho*; Kim exemplified this by having over twenty. According to the *Encyclopedia of Korean Culture*, he had the most art names of any modern Korean poet. An extreme example is that of the Joseon scholar [Kim Jeong-hui](/wiki/Kim_Jeong-hui "Kim Jeong-hui") who, by the estimate of famous calligrapher [Oh Je-bong](/w/index.php?title=Oh_Je-bong&action=edit&redlink=1 "Oh Je-bong (page does not exist)"), had as many as 503. Some people change *ho* to reflect changes in their mood or situation.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

Some *ho* are also exclusively written in the native Korean alphabet Hangul, without corresponding Chinese characters (Hanja). This too could be a symbolic choice. For example, the linguist [Lee Byeong-gi](/wiki/Lee_Byeong-gi "Lee Byeong-gi") chose a pure Hangul name (가람; *Garam*) in part to reflect his sentiments as a [Korean independence activist](/wiki/Korean_independence_movement "Korean independence movement").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

When a person writes an explanation for their *ho*, the explanation is called a *hobyeon* (호변; 號辨) or *hogi* (호기; 號記). A number of texts that catalogue and categorize various *ho* exist, which are called *hobo* (호보; 號譜).[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

#### Types of *ho*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_name&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Types of ho")]

*Ho* can be subdivided into *aho* (아호; 雅號) or *dangho* (당호; 堂號).[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

*Aho* are typical art names (pseudonyms for artists, writers, etc).[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

*Dangho* refers to the name of the building where an intellectual lives. In other words, intellectuals could be referred to by their house's name (it was once common practice for [Korean intellectuals](/wiki/Seonbi "Seonbi") to name their houses). For example, if someone's house was named *Ch'unghyodang* (충효당) and that person went to the park one day, one could use their *dangho* as such: "Ch'unghyodang went to the park." This was sometimes convenient to distinguish between people with similar names. While *dangho* was typically reserved for the owner of the house, it could also be used to refer to other occupants. For example: "the eldest son of Ch'unghyodang".[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Reused names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_name&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reused names")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Art_name "Special:EditPage/Art name") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(September 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In some schools, in particular the main Utagawa school,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the *gō* of the most senior member was adopted when the master died and the chief pupil assumed his position. Perhaps as a sign of respect, artists might take the *gō* of a previous artist. This makes attribution difficult. The censors' seal helps determine a particular print's date. Style also is significant. For example, Kunisada, once he changed his *gō* to Toyokuni, initiated the practice of signing prints with a signature in the elongated oval *toshidama* ('New Year's Jewel') seal of the Utagawa school, an unusual [cartouche](/wiki/Cartouche "Cartouche") with the zig-zag in the upper right-hand corner. His successors continued this practice.

In modern scholarship on the subject, a Roman numeral identifies an artist in the sequence of artists using a *gō*. Thus, Kunisada I is also known as Toyokuni III, since he was the third artist to use that *gō*.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_name&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Courtesy name](/wiki/Courtesy_name "Courtesy name")
* [Pen name](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name")

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Art_name&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Calling someone's name other than *Hao* (i.e. infant name, first name, last name, *[Zi](/wiki/Zi_(name) "Zi (name)")*) without such a person's presentation is seen to be impolite.
